I genuinely dislike how Nike brands the non-ZoomX foams as ZoomX in the shoes that include blends with other EVA’s. They’ve done it on quite a few in the lineup before, but the Vomero is a perfect example. It’s definitely a great shoe, however having that pinky-thin layer of ZoomX underfoot and then branding the midsole with ZoomX on the surface of its EVA carrier foam leads many to believe that it’s a ZoomX chunk there when that’s not the case. They do label them appropriately on the rear of the midsole, but it almost feels like it’s akin to fine print at the bottom of a contract. Not something I think is right, but I’m not a marketing expert and I guess it works for them even if it feels a bit scummy and disingenuous.

The Nike Pegasus Premium is packed with three different types of cushioning: Reactex at the bottom for stability, the first-ever sculpted full-length Zoom Air unit, and ultra-soft ZoomX foam at the top (0:42-0:56).
The Jordan 1 85 Bread had an extremely limited release, with only about 10,000 pairs available originally (1:39-1:42).
Jordan Brand announced an identical Jordan 1 85 Bread pair will be released again before the end of the year, differing only by an extra set of laces (1:52-2:07). This could potentially devalue the original release (2:17-2:25).
The Jordan 1 Nigel Sylvester Better with Time is surprisingly more comfortable than a standard pair of Jordan 1s (3:46-3:54).
The Jordan 5 Black Metallic Reimagined is considered by the speaker to be one of the best reimaginings Jordan Brand has ever done since starting the series in 2020 (5:02-5:09).
The popularity of the Adidas Evo SL largely stems from word-of-mouth rather than collaborations or crazy marketing (6:32-6:37).
The Jordan 4 Undefeated was the first-ever Jordan collaboration, making it a significant piece of sneaker history that paved the way for future collaborations (11:11-11:26).
The Jordan 4 Nigel Sylvester Brick By Brick is ironically named, as "brick" in sneaker resell terms means a shoe that doesn't sell well, but these are "the farthest things from bricks" (12:19-12:25).
The word "best" is said 7 times in the video (0:14, 0:25, 5:02, 6:01, 7:44, 11:00, 11:47).

My immediate reaction was, that feels synthetic. It does become really soft after you wear them for a bit and warm them up, but again, no where near like a good leather upper. Because of the natural tension you'll always notice it. Touch on the ball was numb. I don't know if this improves over time but I didn't like it. Insole is actually the best nike have done in my experience. It a relatively thick ortholite foam which does a good job of absorption. Miles better than the other rubbish they use in their elite boots. The heel is the biggest problem for me. It's horrendous. It's not the width thats the problem. There more cushioning towards the base of the Achilles rather than higher up, so there's a painful pressure point there. Also the shape and the smooth suede lining means my foot feels insecure and is sliding round. The Diadora Sala Elite Tf is also really wide but has the best heel I've tried in a boot. My absolute favorite shoes of 2025 are the Nike V+ and the Zoom Fly 6. I use the V+ for long and easy runs, and then the ZF6 for tempo sessions. The softness under the forefoot with the V+, and the way it gives you that bounce, is such an amazing feeling that running in them is pure enjoyment.

The ZF6, on the other hand, is a rocket — incredibly fast shoes. In my opinion they’re actually faster than the Alphafly 3 because of their geometry. I’ve noticed that you use the term “simplistic” instead of simple alot which might be misleading as it has a different negative meaning.

The EVO SL is not a “takedown” of the Adios Pro EVO, and repeating that does a disservice to both shoes.

AEPro is an elite Race Day ONLY shoe. It features a dedicated foam compound. The upper, midsole, and outsole are also dedicated to the model.

EVO SL is a “works for most” Daily Trainer with engineering that makes it suitable for sped workouts and some racing. It has a midsole with similar geometry that makes up for the absence of Energy Rods, but it features a dogbone torsion shank for stability. The midsole compound is actually the same as the Adios Pro 3. The upper is a blend previous SL models (SL20, SL2) and new upper tech.

There are more similarities between the Adios Pro 3 and EVO SL. But the EVO SL is not a takedown of anything. Adizero currently only has the SL2 and EVO SL slotted as its training options. EVO SL is widely considered 2025 Top 3 Daily Trainers across all brands.

The problem with modern running shoes like the Vomero Plus and Vomero 18 in the list, and others like any On shoe, or the Adidas Evo SL, and more is that the foam is super soft and squishy that it will cause instability and will cause problems with your achilles and overall foot health in the long run if you keep wearing them for walking all day everyday. This happens because it’s either your heel or forefoot that will sink in more into the foam, cause your foot to be pointed upwards or downwards which stretches and stresses your achilles tendon. You may not feel it, but it’s happening. Plus the very curved rocker shape of those two plus most modern running shoes are also bad for your feet. They’re not like the retro runners from Nike, New Balance, Asics and others where the foam is a lot stiffer and the midsole shape is a lot more flat and not curved, creating a flat, stable, and supportive base where your feet wont sink into the foam (or air units in the case of retro Air Max shoes like the 95s or Air Max 1). This is why if I know I’m gonna be walking all day I grab my New Balance 990V6, Air Max 1 big bubble or now the Air Max 95, cause those shoes are neutral and much more supportive than modern running shoes while still providing enough give and comfort. The only time where these ultra soft and squishy foams will be good for you is if you have foot problems like forefoot pain or plantar fasciitis. But otherwise, even the likes of the Air Force 1s, Jordan 1s, any classic Vans model, Chucks, Superstars are actually better for your overall foot health. Also, notice how Nike used React X in the Ava Rover, a shoe they made specifically for walking in the city all day? That’s cause React X is nowhere near as soft and squishy as Zoom X, a foam made specifically for modern day running shoes.

And yes, I didn’t mention the Pegasus premium as part of the problematic shoe because the react x and full length zoom air units in those actually create a stable, supportive and stiff enough platform so your foot won’t sink into the zoom x foam as much as if it were just zoom x foam alone.

I would dare to say that the Nike mistake cost them billions at this point.

But there are two MAJOR points that you do not mention.

1) The problem with trying to start a new basketball 🏀 driven sports brand is that basketball no longer drives shoe sales. Not in the mainstream. There has been a zeitgeist shift. Forget about sneaker heads and basketball fans. Look at what the average person ....the mainstream.....is mostly wearing these days. Running shoes. It's now running that is predominantly driving shoe sales. Basketball shoes are now like Soccer shoes. Very technical and specialised sports equipment. No longer suited for wearing all day standing or walking. That was a huge oversight by UA when deciding to start Curry brand in the first place. UA really over invested in basketball.

2) Stephan Curry is not Michael Jordan. He is a basketball super star but he certainly has not transcended the sport. Everyone in the world knew who Michael Jordan was. Even in all the countries where basketball is not popular or well known. Stephan Curry is largely unknown outside countries where basketball is popular. So Jordan has been able to be sold and marketed globally. That just isn't true of Curry brand. Curry brand would always be confined to certain markets.

Curry brand was never going to be Jordan (in terms of success ) regardless of who it was with.

The name "Under Armour" will forever shoebox the brand as a performance brand. No matter their products, quality, marketing, or business practices, the brand's name is an antonym for casual lifestyle wear. They will always fail in the lifestyle segment because of their name.

Nike, Adidas, New Balance, etc are neutral names that work well for both performance products and lifestyle products. "Under Armour" is too far an aggressive and sports oriented name to be attached to anything other than performance products. No one wants to wear Under Armour to a casual dinner, a night at the movies, or hanging out with friends. The name and brand has too strong of an association to sports and the gym.
